title: Stock signal component decomposition and conviction quality
observation: The market-level signal already returns a full 'components' dict showing each factor's contribution (crash_prob, regime, valuation, momentum, etc.). However, get_stock_signal returns only the final composite_score — users cannot see WHY a stock is Buy vs Hold, or what drives its specific rating. All stock-specific adjustments (analyst target, momentum, drawdown, PE, options, earnings, crash risk) are opaque. Two stocks with the same composite score of +0.3 could have completely different driver profiles — one from strong momentum, another from low PE + analyst upside — but there's no way to distinguish them.
what_i_did: Refactored get_stock_signal() to track each component's score contribution individually

Added 'components' dict to stock signal output with 10 named components: market_base, beta_adjustment, analyst_target, sector_momentum, valuation (PE + earnings growth combined), crash_risk, drawdown, momentum, options, earnings
Added _compute_conviction_quality() function that measures cross-component agreement
Conviction quality classifies signals as high/moderate/low based on whether components agree or conflict
Added 'conviction' dict to stock signal output with quality level, agreement_pct, dominant_driver, n_contributing
Wired new fields through screener endpoint (signal_components, signal_conviction) and single-stock endpoint
Wrote 31 targeted tests covering component structure, attribution correctness, conviction quality classification, backward compatibility, and stock differentiation

before.conviction_metric: none — confidence = abs(score) * 100, no cross-component agreement

after.component_visibility: full — 10 named components with individual contributions
after.example_low_conviction: Mixed inputs (bearish analyst + bullish momentum + bearish drawdown) → quality=low/moderate

analysis: This is a transparency/interpretability improvement, not a signal accuracy change. The composite score computation is identical — I only added instrumentation to track what was already being computed. The conviction quality metric adds genuinely new information: it distinguishes between high-confidence consensus signals (where all factors agree) and low-confidence tug-of-war signals (where the composite happens to be positive but components are conflicting). This is directly useful for users deciding whether to act on a signal.
self_critique: The conviction quality thresholds (75% = high, 50% = moderate) are somewhat arbitrary — they should ideally be calibrated against backtest data to verify that high-conviction signals actually perform better than low-conviction ones
I did not add the component decomposition to the backtest evaluation — a future cycle could test whether filtering by conviction quality improves backtest hit rates
The screener endpoint now returns more data per stock, which slightly increases payload size — this is negligible for 20-30 stocks but worth noting
I didn't touch the frontend — the new data is available in the API but not displayed yet
next_steps: Backtest conviction quality: filter historical signals by conviction level and compare hit rates (do high-conviction signals actually perform better?)
Frontend: display component breakdown on stock detail page as a waterfall chart
Add conviction_quality to signal_analytics for screener-level aggregation
Calibrate conviction thresholds against backtest data
depth_rating: medium — clean instrumentation of existing computation, new conviction metric, thorough tests, but no accuracy improvement
